section.title: Widows, Elders, and Church Order
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns to the practical ordering of the church as a family. Different ages are to be treated with familial respect; true widows are to be honored and cared for, while families bear first responsibility for their own; elders who lead well deserve double honor, yet are subject to fair discipline. The chapter shows the household of God caring justly and tenderly for its members.
section.thesis: The church is God's family, so it must honor and provide for the truly needy, uphold the duty of families to care for their own, and treat its leaders with both generous honor and impartial accountability.
section.frameNote: Five movements: treating each age as family; honoring true widows and the duty of relatives; the enrollment of widows; double honor and fair discipline for elders; and a solemn charge to Timothy on impartiality and purity.

verses.text: Do not rebuke an older man, but appeal to him as to a father. Treat younger men as brothers,
older women as mothers, and younger women as sisters, with absolute purity.
Honor the {{chera|widows}} who are truly widows.
But if a widow has children or grandchildren, they must first learn to show {{eusebeo|godliness}} to their own family and repay their parents, for this is pleasing in the sight of God.
The widow who is truly in need and left all alone puts her hope in God and continues night and day in her petitions and prayers.
But she who lives for pleasure is dead even while she is still alive.
Give these instructions to the believers, so that they will be above reproach.
If anyone does not provide for his own, and especially his own household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.
A widow should be enrolled if she is at least sixty years old, faithful to her husband,
and well known for good deeds such as bringing up children, entertaining strangers, washing the feet of the saints, imparting relief to the afflicted, and devoting herself to every good work.
But refuse to enroll younger widows. For when their passions draw them away from Christ, they will want to marry,
and thus will incur judgment because they are setting aside their first faith.
At the same time they will also learn to be idle, going from house to house and being not only idle, but also gossips and busybodies, discussing things they should not mention.
So I advise the younger widows to marry, have children, and manage their households, denying the adversary occasion for slander.
For some have already turned aside to follow Satan.
If any believing woman has dependent widows, she must assist them and not allow the church to be burdened, so that it can help the widows who are truly in need.
{{presbyteros|Elders}} who lead effectively are worthy of double {{time|honor}}, especially those who work hard at preaching and teaching.
For the {{graphe|Scripture}} says, “Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain,” and, “The worker is worthy of his wages.”
Do not entertain an accusation against an elder, except on the testimony of two or three witnesses.
But those who persist in sin should be rebuked in front of everyone, so that the others will stand in fear of sin.
I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus and the elect angels to maintain these principles without bias, and to do nothing out of partiality.
Do not be too quick in the laying on of hands and thereby share in the sins of others. Keep yourself pure.
Stop drinking only water and use a little wine instead, because of your stomach and your frequent ailments.
The sins of some men are obvious, going ahead of them to judgment; but the sins of others do not surface until later.
In the same way, good deeds are obvious, and even the ones that are inconspicuous cannot remain hidden.

chera.gloss: A widow, a woman who has lost her husband. Paul distinguishes those who are truly widows, alone and in need, from those with family able to help. The care of widows reaches back to God's special concern for them in the law and prophets and continues in the early church.

eusebeo.gloss: To show godliness or reverence, here toward one's own family; the verb form of eusebeia, godliness. Caring for aging parents and relatives is not a lesser, worldly duty but an expression of true godliness, pleasing in the sight of God.

presbyteros.gloss: An elder. The same word means an older man, as in verse 1, and the church office of elder, as here in verse 17. In the office sense it names the leaders who govern and teach, worthy of double honor when they lead well, especially those who labor in preaching.

time.gloss: Honor, respect, and by extension the material support that honor carries. Double honor for hardworking elders includes both esteem and financial provision, as the two quoted Scriptures about the ox and the worker's wages make clear.

graphe.gloss: Scripture, a writing regarded as God's word. Strikingly, Paul introduces both a quotation from Deuteronomy and a saying of Jesus with the Scripture says, placing the words of Jesus alongside the Old Testament as authoritative Scripture.

basic.context: Paul tells Timothy to treat the church as a family: older men as fathers, younger women as sisters, and so on. He gives careful instructions on widows, honoring those truly alone while insisting families care for their own. He gives wise counsel about younger widows, then turns to elders: those who lead well deserve double honor, but accusations must be tested and persistent sin rebuked openly. He charges Timothy to be impartial and to keep himself pure.
basic.hermeneutics: This is mostly practical wisdom for ordering church life, and its principles carry over even where the specific arrangements were shaped by that culture. The abiding truths stand out: honor and provide for the truly needy, families must care for their own, and leaders deserve both generous support and impartial accountability.
basic.originalLanguages: A chera, widow, truly alone is to be honored, while relatives must eusebeo, show godliness, to their own. Presbyteros means both older man and elder; elders who lead well deserve double time, honor. Paul cites the graphe, Scripture, quoting both Moses and Jesus as authoritative.
basic.typology: God's care for the widow, woven through the law and prophets, points to Christ who has compassion on the helpless and to the church as His family that cares for its own. The elder who labors in the word serves under Christ the Chief Shepherd.
basic.christ: The elders who work hard at preaching and teaching serve Christ's flock, and their support rests on Jesus' own word that the worker is worthy of his wages. The charge is given before God and Christ Jesus, whose gaze governs how the church is to be led.
basic.god: God is deeply concerned for the vulnerable and is pleased when families honor their own. He is the one before whom Timothy is charged, watching over the church's fairness. Care for widows and honesty in leadership are matters that touch His own heart and honor.
basic.crossReferences: James 1 defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows. Deuteronomy 25 and Luke 10 stand behind verse 18. Acts 6 shows the early church organizing to care for widows.
basic.oneStory: God has always defended the widow, the orphan, and the poor, and called His people to reflect His compassion. Here the church is ordered as a caring family under accountable leaders. It is part of God's long work of forming a people whose life together shows His justice and mercy, gathered under Christ the Shepherd.

context.literary: Chapter 5 moves through distinct groups: the ages of the congregation, widows, and elders, before a solemn personal charge to Timothy. The teaching on widows is the longest, reflecting a real pastoral crisis, and the section on elders balances honor with discipline. Verse 23, on Timothy's health, is a brief personal aside.
context.historical: Widows without family support were among the most vulnerable people in the ancient world, with few means of income. The early church maintained a recognized list, or order, of widows who received support and gave themselves to prayer and service. Elders in some churches were supported financially so they could labor in the word.
context.canonical: The chapter continues Scripture's long concern for widows, from the Mosaic law to Acts 6 and James 1. It stands as a model of ordered compassion, holding together generous care, family responsibility, and accountable leadership.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To guide Timothy in ordering the church as a family: caring rightly for widows, holding families to their duty, honoring and disciplining elders fairly, and leading with impartiality and personal purity.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The abiding principles are prescriptive: honor and provide for the truly needy, families care for their own, leaders receive honor and impartial accountability. The specific mechanisms, an enrolled list of widows sixty and older, reflect that setting and are applied wisely rather than woodenly today.

debates.question: What is the enrollment of widows in verses 9 to 16?

views.label: A support list for the church's care
views.case: The enrolled widows are those the church commits to support financially because they are truly alone and qualified by a life of good works.

views.label: A recognized order of ministry
views.case: Some see a defined order of older widows pledged to prayer and service, which is why a pledge-breaking desire to remarry raised concern, so younger widows were not enrolled.

debates.bearing: Either way the church balances generous care for the truly needy with wisdom about commitments, urging younger widows toward marriage and family rather than idleness.

debates.question: Why does Paul mention wine for Timothy's stomach in verse 23?

views.label: Practical pastoral care for his health
views.case: A simple, tender instruction about a real ailment, showing Paul's fatherly concern amid weighty commands.

views.label: A balance to keep yourself pure
views.case: Coming right after keep yourself pure, it guards against an over-strict asceticism, reminding Timothy that purity is not the same as needless self-denial that harms the body.

debates.bearing: Both readings show a wisdom that neither indulges the body nor punishes it, receiving God's provision, including medicine, with good sense.

study.originalLanguages: The chera, widow, who is truly alone is to be honored and supported, while her relatives must eusebeo, practice godliness, toward their own family, a duty pleasing to God. Paul plays on presbyteros, which means both an older man, verse 1, and the church office of elder, verse 17; elders who lead well merit double time, honor, a word covering both esteem and material support. To ground that support Paul cites the graphe, Scripture, quoting Deuteronomy on the unmuzzled ox and, remarkably, a saying of Jesus, the worker is worthy of his wages, treating the Lord's words as Scripture alongside Moses.

typology.type: God's defense of the widow and the helpless
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's compassion on the vulnerable and in the church as His family that cares for its own.

typology.type: The laboring elder worthy of his wages
typology.fulfillment: The undershepherd who serves under Christ the Chief Shepherd, supported so he can labor in the word.

study.christ: The elders who work hard at preaching and teaching feed Christ's flock, and their right to support rests on Jesus' own word, quoted as Scripture, that the worker is worthy of his wages. The whole charge is given before God and Christ Jesus and the elect angels, setting the church's fairness under the eye of the Lord. In caring for the vulnerable and leading with integrity, the church displays the compassion and justice of Christ its Head.
study.god: God is the one who is pleased when families honor their own and who watches over the church's justice. He has always been the defender of the widow and the fatherless, and He is the God before whom Timothy is solemnly charged to act without partiality. Care for the needy and honesty in leadership are not mere administration but matters that touch God's own character and honor.

crossReferences.note: Pure and undefiled religion is to care for orphans and widows in their distress.
Do not muzzle an ox while it treads the grain, cited in verse 18 for the elder's support.
The worker is worthy of his wages, Jesus' word cited by Paul as Scripture.
The early church organizing the daily care of widows so that the ministry of the word could continue.

study.oneStory: God has always defended the widow, the orphan, and the poor, and called His people to mirror His compassion and justice. Here the church is ordered as a caring family led by accountable elders. This ordered mercy belongs to God's long work of forming a people whose shared life shows His heart for the vulnerable and His truthfulness in leadership, all gathered under Christ the Shepherd.

quiz.q: How does Paul tell Timothy to relate to different members of the church?

options.text: As family: older men as fathers, younger women as sisters, and so on.
options.note: Verses 1 and 2 frame the church as a family to be treated with familial respect.

options.text: As employees under a strict manager.
options.note: The image is family, not a workplace hierarchy.

options.text: As strangers to keep at a distance.
options.note: Paul urges warm, familial treatment, not distance.

options.text: As rivals to be kept in check.
options.note: They are family to be honored, not rivals.

quiz.q: Who bears first responsibility for a widow with living family?

options.text: Her own children and grandchildren, as an act of godliness.
options.note: Verse 4 places the first duty on the family.

options.text: The state, with no role for the family.
options.note: Paul assigns the primary duty to relatives, not to outsiders.

options.text: The church alone, regardless of family.
options.note: The church helps the truly alone; families care for their own first.

options.text: No one; she must fend for herself.
options.note: Scripture insists she be cared for, first by family.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about someone who will not provide for his own household?

options.text: He has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.
options.note: Verse 8 states this strong warning.

options.text: He has simply made an understandable choice.
options.note: Paul treats it as a grave denial of the faith, not a neutral choice.

options.text: He is more spiritual for trusting God to provide.
options.note: Neglect of family is condemned, not commended as faith.

options.text: It makes no difference to his standing.
options.note: Paul says it amounts to denying the faith.

quiz.q: What are elders who lead well worthy of?

options.text: Double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching.
options.note: Verse 17 calls for double honor, including support.

options.text: Unquestioning obedience with no accountability.
options.note: They are honored, yet still subject to fair discipline, verses 19 and 20.

options.text: Suspicion and constant accusation.
options.note: Accusations require two or three witnesses; they are not to be entertained lightly.

options.text: Nothing beyond a bare thank-you.
options.note: Paul calls for double honor, including material provision.

quiz.q: How is Timothy to handle accusations and discipline among leaders?

options.text: Without bias or partiality, testing accusations by two or three witnesses.
options.note: Verses 19 to 21 require witnesses and impartiality.

options.text: By believing every accusation at once.
options.note: Accusations need witnesses; they are not to be entertained lightly.

options.text: By favoring his friends and allies.
options.note: He is charged to do nothing out of partiality.

options.text: By ignoring persistent sin to keep the peace.
options.note: Persistent sin is to be rebuked openly, verse 20.

questions: Paul treats the church as a family. Who in your congregation could you honor and care for more as a genuine father, mother, brother, or sister?
Caring for aging parents is called godliness pleasing to God. How are you carrying, or neglecting, that duty toward your own family right now?
The lonely widow put her hope in God and prayed night and day. What might her example teach you about turning need into prayer?
Elders who labor in the word deserve double honor. How well does your church honor and support those who preach and teach, and how do you play a part?
Timothy was charged to act without partiality. Where are you tempted to let friendship or bias shape a judgment that should be fair?

translationNotes.note: Older man renders presbyteros, the same word translated elder in verse 17; here it means an older man by age, not the church office.
Double honor translates a phrase carrying both esteem and material support, as the quotations in verse 18 confirm.
The two quotations are Deuteronomy 25:4 and Luke 10:7; Paul introduces both with the Scripture says, treating Jesus' words as Scripture.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 5:1-25 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
